facts.context: Nagel claims that most skeptical arguments:
facts.truth: grow from the consistent application of ordinary standards.
distractors: are the result of applying arbitrarily stringent standards.
are based on linguistic confusions.
are logically self-refuting.

facts.context: According to Kant, morality requires us to:
facts.truth: act only on maxims that we can will to become universal laws.
distractors: perform the action that leads to the greatest total happiness.
behave only in such a way as a perfectly virtuous person would behave.
place the interests of others above the interests of ourselves.

facts.context: Hume divides our perceptions into two categories:
facts.truth: impressions and ideas.
distractors: sensations and emotions.
static and dynamic.
simple and complex.

facts.context: Epicurus claims that all other virtues spring from:
facts.truth: prudence.
distractors: temperance.
justice.
courage.

facts.context: Concerning the relationship between morality and theology, Bentham claims that:
facts.truth: we must first know whether something is right before we can know whether it conforms to God’s will.
distractors: we must first know whether something conforms to God’s will before we can know that it is right.
God exists, but does not concern himself with matters of morality.
God does not exist.

facts.context: "Is there anything you would be willing to die for?" is a philosophical question insofar as:
facts.truth: it forces us to articulate and justify our beliefs about what we know and ought to do.
distractors: it does not have any right or wrong answer because it is a meaningless question.
it is a meaningless question because everyone could have a different answer to it.
it is more concerned with one's religious beliefs than with factual claims about the world.

facts.context: According to Baier’s theory, the second step in assessing whether an action is morally permissible is to find out:
facts.truth: whether the moral rule forbidding it is a genuine moral rule.
distractors: whether it is forbidden by a moral rule of one’s group.
whether it is forbidden by a law.
whether one can approve of it.

facts.context: Wolf claims that ultimately, normative questions must be assessed from:
facts.truth: a perspective that is unattached to a commitment to any well-ordered system of values.
distractors: the moral point of view.
the point of view of virtue.
the point of view of individual perfection.

facts.context: According to Parfit, both Taurek and Lewis assume that for there to be a “sum of pain,” it must be:
facts.truth: all felt by a single person.
distractors: all felt at a single time.
all be caused by a single source.
all of the above.

facts.context: An important formal characteristic of art is_____.
facts.truth: coherence
distractors: social meaning
faithful representation
its power to evoke strong emotions

facts.context: According to Hobbes, in the condition of man in which there is a state of war of everyone against everyone, every man has a right to _____.
facts.truth: everything
distractors: some things
lawful treatment
due process

facts.context: Baier claims that ethical skepticism is often due to:
facts.truth: confused epistemological theories.
distractors: confused metaphysical theories.
confused religious views.
confused scientific theories.

facts.context: How many categories of ethical principles do Diener and Crandell refer to?
facts.truth: Four: harm to participants; lack of informed consent; invasion of privacy; involvement of deception
distractors: Three: harm to students; lack of informed consent; invasion of privacy on social media.
Two: ethical and unethical
Two: right and wrong

facts.context: Augustine identifies evil with:
facts.truth: the absence of good.
distractors: the influence of the devil.
a unique force, opposed to goodness.
pain.

facts.context: Aquinas says that the first efficient cause of everything is _____.
facts.truth: God
distractors: the universe
nature
an infinite series

facts.context: Moore claims that there is no meaning in saying that pleasure is good, unless:
facts.truth: good is something different than pleasure.
distractors: goodness and pleasure are the same thing.
all good things are pleasant.
all pleasant things are good.

facts.context: Nussbaum claims that the specification of particular virtues:
facts.truth: might sometimes turn out to be a disjunction.
distractors: will always consist of a single answer.
is necessarily relative to a culture.
can be settled in such a way as to be no longer revisable.

facts.context: According to Rawls, we can enter the original position:
facts.truth: at any time, by simply following a certain procedure.
distractors: only by leaving society and remaking social institutions from scratch.
only if we somehow forget who we are, which is very rare.
none of the above.

facts.context: According to Gauthier, deliberative justification:
facts.truth: ignores morality and seemingly replaces it.
distractors: is refuted by moral requirements.
is replaced once we acknowledge moral requirements.
refutes morality.

facts.context: In response to the argument that famine relief is counterproductive because it leads to overpopulation, Singer claims that:
facts.truth: this is a reason to give money to population control efforts.
distractors: this has empirically been shown to be false.
this is true, but should not be of moral concern to us.
there is no such thing as “overpopulation.”

facts.context: What was Schopenhauer's attitude toward life?
facts.truth: He thought life was bereft of meaning.
distractors: He thought life was uncertain but worthwhile.
He thought life was hard but ultimately hopeful.
He believed in an afterlife in which all misery vanishes.

facts.context: Gauthier claims that moral agreements that are equally favorable to all parties are desirable because they:
facts.truth: satisfy our desire for fairness.
distractors: satisfy the conceptual constraint against exploitation.
maximize the productivity of society.
invite the stable compliance of everyone.

facts.context: According to Rawls, the two parts of his theory:
facts.truth: are independent: You can accept either part without accepting the other.
distractors: are interdependent: You cannot accept one part without accepting the other.
are provable from self-evident premises.
cannot be justified via rational means.

facts.context: Epictetus claims that when someone strikes you, what really angers you is:
facts.truth: your own judgment.
distractors: the force of the blow.
their malicious intentions.
the insult involved.

facts.context: Hume describes reason as:
facts.truth: cool and disengaged.
distractors: the source of all moral actions.
the first spring or impulse to desire.
all of the above.

facts.context: What are some of the frequent frustrations in writing or reading about research ethics?
facts.truth: All of the above.
distractors: Writers differ over what is ethically acceptable.
The same debates are rehearsed over decades.
Cases of ethical violation tend to be linked with certain research methods.

facts.context: According to Cicero, immorality is __________, and expediency is __________.
facts.truth: repugnant to Nature; in accord with Nature.
distractors: repugnant to Nature; repugnant to Nature.
in accord with Nature; repugnant to Nature.
in accord with Nature; in accord with Nature.

facts.context: Da Vinci's Mona Lisa is_____.
facts.truth: representational
distractors: abstract
obscure
nonrepresentational

facts.context: One example of bad constitutive moral luck would be:
facts.truth: a person who is by nature envious and ungenerous.
distractors: a person who lived in Nazi Germany in 1940.
a person who drives drunk and hits a child.
all of the above.

facts.context: Craig says an actually infinite number of things _____.
facts.truth: cannot exist
distractors: is possible
is meaningless
exists somewhere
